transcript.transcript: June Powers This is March 24, 1989. My name is June Powers. Today I'll be talking with Harry Kinney, a lifelong resident of the Vague community in Bonner County, Idaho. We'll talk of early years and changes in the area. Good morning, Harry. I'm Harry Kenny. I was born July the 17th, 1921, right here on the place where I now live. And your parents, that was 1921. 1921. Yeah. Your parents came here, your mother came before the turn of the century, right? Well, no. She came in 1900. Oh, 1900. Tell me about what you have heard about the time when she came. Well, when she came, they got off the train about a mile north of Laclede and crossed the river on the ice on Thanksgiving Day in 1900. Did they come right out to this part of the country then? Her grandfather lived down here about a mile and a half where Betty Bell lives now in the house there by the creek. And then my grandmother took up a stone and timber claim, kind of a homestead. And they lived in a log house there for, I don't know, three or four years. Four years, probably. And then my grandmother married another man. They lived over here about, west of here about a mile. Your grandmother's name when she first came here was what? Wilson. Mary Wilson. And who did she marry later here? Iram Jenkins. And they had three daughters. Iram Jenkins. My mother had two brothers and two sisters. What was your mother's name? Wilson. And somebody mentioned the other day, Gurdum. Gurdum. Gurdum was my mother's great uncle that homesteaded this place right here. He was a judge here in this valley at one time. Justice and Peace. Or something. Yeah. The other day when we talked about crossing on the ice, Hazel said that your mother later said If she had anything to do with it, she wouldn't have wanted to go out. It's a good thing she was only one year older. She never got out on that ice. She was kind of scared of the water. And then your father's name was what? My father came in 1911 from Canada. Came in here from Canada with his family. And they bought this place here, this 160 acres here, from Charles Gurdum. And his name was what? Patrick. Well, my grandfather's name was William Kenny. And his father was Patrick. My father was Patrick. He was about 14 years old when he came here. Tell us a little bit about what the area was like when you first remember it. Well, when I first remember it, it was quite a bit of a lumber company was doing lots of logging around here. They had probably three or four camps around here pretty close to the Edgemere area. And my dad sawed for them one winter here. I think that was probably about 27. That was after the fire went through in 26. He sawed, for him, up here one winter, west of Edgemere. And he drove back and forth there in an old Model T. But a few days, the weather was so bad that he had to walk. And he left his car sitting in the shed there. My brother was about four years old, and he'd seen him put air in the tires, so he took the caps off and let all the air out of the whole four tires on that old Model T. My dad, he couldn't figure it out. He thought all the tires went flat at once, so he took them all off and There was nothing wrong with them. Oh, God. Yeah, buddy. It's too much. Then you went to school right here in the area at? I went to school down here city act teams about Little over a mile about a mile and a half from here. You went to go walk? Went there for eight years While I was going to school there, in the fall, the Indians used to come over here and set up the teepee, a couple of teepees and hunt here in the fall. And I'd been to school. I knew what they'd done to you. So I went way out and around through the field to get rid of them. Excuse me. So Bill, my brother, He sneaked right up behind them as close as he could get behind the trees and watched them. He didn't know what they might do to you. Oh, I think that's so funny. But that's true. We do have this preconceived notion of what's about the Indians. Yeah, well, I always figured they'd run off with either a skelpie or something. How many years? Did they come here for several years? Yeah, that was three or four years they'd come here. I suppose it'd be long in October, November, and spend about 10 days over there hunting. I don't know what they, I think it was just deer they killed. Do you remember who the teachers were, or much about the teachers? Oh, yeah. I remember the teachers. My first teacher, his name was Bill Kenny. Spelled a little different than mine do, what my name is. His wife still lives up here at Sandpoint. Bill was an assessor for a good many years here. Then my second teacher was A girl by the name of Catherine Schmidt. She was from up north at Sandpoint. And then we had Nita Hanson taught here for a good many years. She taught three years here at Cineac Dean. And then Yvonne Mead was my teacher in the eighth grade. And when I was in that first grade, she was in the eighth grade. And then she came back and taught me later when I was in the eighth grade. And your mother's family came here. You said your dad came from Canada. Your mother's family came from? Minnesota. Minnesota. And specifically, what brought them here, you think? Just Homestead land, I guess. I haven't ever really heard him say. And then they began farming here? Yeah, they cleared up small farms here. But most of my mother's, most of her family were loggers. One of her uncles, his name was Frank Horn. He lived in Priest River for many years. He was a Woodsman down there. What was his last name? Warren? Warren. How do you spell that? H-O-R-N. H-O-R-N. Warren. Warren. So then you went into logging, too, then, as soon as It was time to go to work. I spent my whole life at it, 50 years so far. Were they logging trees right around this Bay Area when you first started logging? Well, no, not right around here. This country had been pretty well logged off at that time. And when I first started working, it was about 50 miles from here in Caribou Basin. I was 17 years old. But in later years now, this timber in this country's grown back. They log pretty heavy in here now. What was the logging procedure like when you first started? Well, when I first started, they were still logging pretty much horses. But then in the next three or four years, why, they pretty well converted over to horses, from horses to jammer and cat log. The first year I saw it at Long Lake, they had 22 teams working there. The Next year, I think they had seven. And then after that, they only had one. Well, now, for someone like me who knows absolutely nothing about teams of horses, could you explain a little bit about how they did that, what a team of horses consisted of, and how you had to take care of them, or any part of it that you recall knowing about in detail? Well, a team of horses consisted of two horses and a man to drive them. And they were pretty fussy those days. They wanted your timber all fell up and down the hill so they could get it with those horses. And the horses wouldn't have to turn those logs sideways on the sideways position on the hill. They didn't want them to fell cross-legged to the hill. And how did they attach to the logs, sort of like the? Well, some of them sometimes used chains. And then most of the time, they had a pair of tongs that they'd just snap onto the logs and drag them down with that. And where did they take them to, and what did they do with them when they got in there? Well, when there was a logload of horses, they usually had skidways built. They'd skid them down those skidways and deck them up, and then load them on the trucks. So the team of horses would go alongside the skidway, and the logs would go in the skidway? No, they'd just take them down to these skidways. And when they got there, what, a teamster or whoever was there would roll these logs on the skid ways and pile them up. It's hard to picture it if you've never seen it. By the time they started skidding with jammers, why, they just decked them up anywhere, usually on the lower side of the road, down below the road. Now, what's a jammer? Most of them are truck mounted. They have a mast and a boom and a set of drums that brings the log in from the woods to the road. And then there's a haulback that takes the tongs back out to the woods. And they skid a lot of logs on. Were they still in the area you were in, floating any logs in the water, along the rivers or anything? I never did ever work where they drove any logs down the river, but they were still doing it here on Priest River. They drove the Priest River for, I'm imagining, up until about the 40s, sometime in the late 40s. My uncle, this Frank Horn, he used to be in charge of that drive coming down the Preacher River quite often. He was an old river man. I bet that'd be kind of fascinating. Yeah. It's pretty rough life out there this time of the year in that cold water. Well, then how did they move the logs where you were working once they were decked? They were loaded on trucks and hauled to a landing at Colburn, Idaho. And then they were loaded onto log cars and shipped into Long Lake Mill in Spokane. So you, And the Long Lake Mill then was where? That particular mill that we're talking about was right there on Post Street in Spokane, just right downtown Spokane is where it was at. And we hauled the logs in and unloaded them right along just across the river from that big clock. There used to be in the old Union, no, the Great Northern Depot. The landing was right there where that expo was held here, what, ten, twelve years ago. That was what they called the old Phoenix Mill. I guess that was about the oldest mill around Spokane. Phoenix? Phoenix. Well, then you helped haul to them? Yeah, we went. You drove truck? No, I didn't drive truck, but we used to go in there. Most times, it was truck in the evening. Well, along Lake Camp where I started working was about, oh, I imagine there was about 125 men worked there, and there was eight, there was about eight pretty big bunkhouses held about 12 to 16 men, and then there was quite a few smaller ones. And then there was a big cookhouse where they must have had three cooks there, and a dishwasher and about Three flunkies, I think. They were the waiters. They brought the groceries to the table for the loggers. And then they had an office and blacksmith shops and a pretty big barn for 20 teams, 40 head of horses. And they didn't have any recreation halls or anything for the men, I guess like they did in some places. What were the buildings made of? Lumber. Yeah, I was wondering if they might have built them of logs. Well, I think if I remember right, the barn was built out of logs. Most of them were just little framed buildings. There were a lot of young people your age. You said earlier you were about 17 when you went to Washington. Well, I was 17. I was about the youngest one working there. Most of them were older guys. It was the time I started working the woods way. The old lumberjacks, a lot of them would go in in the spring and they'd stay there until about the Fourth of July, and then they'd either quit and go to town or they'd go to town and have a big celebration and then come back. But a lot of them changed camps, go to another camp somewhere and work after the Fourth of July. But about the time I started in there, why, the younger generation started working. They weren't the old lumberjacks like the old-timers were. Who were some of these people? Were they from all over the country? A lot of them used to come up from this country, from Spokane. And in the winter they'd go back down to Trent and live down there in Trent. Trent is what they called the skid row there in Spokane. They've cleaned that up now, and Trent's a pretty good place, And the Winos have moved up another part of town. Are you saying then that a lot of the loggers Didn't have homes that they went to then? No. A lot of them just went into Spokane and stayed in the wintertime there in those cheap hotels. And they'd come out and spend the summers in the lawn camp. Did you get up and start early at the crack of dawn? We usually got up about 5.30, wasn't the crack of dawn. So what was your time? They'd have a bell ring about 5.30, and you'd get up and go over to the cookhouse and put up your lunches then. Then about 6, why, breakfast bell would ring, and you'd go over and eat breakfast and then go to the woods and work after that. So what did you actually do out in the woods? Take an axe and chop down a tree? Well, we sawed them. We carried an axe and sawed them. Usually three or four wedges and a hammer. What kind of saws? Well, they were crosscut saws. They usually used Royal Chinooks, they called them, or the Atkins. How long were they? Six feet. They gave you a lot of good exercise. Did you already know how to use all of the tools before you went to the camp? Oh, yes. You had learned that at home? Oh, yeah. I grew up with that. What were some funny incidents that you remember happening in the camp? Well, one of them, my cub bear come into camp one night and a bunch of guys chased him up a snag. He come up about 20 feet and they started pounding on the snag with the sticks and everything and it tickled his feet and he jumped right down in the middle of them and that's pretty good. And then they set a snare one night in the garbage can out a ways from the cookhouse and run a piece of telephone wire over and put on the fellow, the leg of his bunk, his bed. He discovered it and wondered what the wire was and followed it out to the garbage can. And there was a snare there they had fixed up for the bear. But he never got into it, so he didn't take no ride in his bed. I don't remember too much. Did they give you a hard time because you were the youngster in the group? Oh, no. They accepted you the same as everybody else? Oh, yeah. Well, there was other guys working there who were pretty young, but I think I was about the youngest who was sawing. I think old Buster Walson, I think he was hauling logs out of there with a truck when he was 14 or 15. Back to this part of the country, Over the period of years that you've lived here, there's been a lot of changes from when you were a boy. Are there more people now than there were? There is more people here now than there was when I was a boy. But even at the time I was a boy growing up, there was quite a few people that lived around here. When I went to school down here, there was some years, there was a few with seven kids in the school there. And then a couple of years later, there might be 40. So the people came and went. And then when That drought hit in the Midwest in the, I suppose it was in the early 30s. About 35 and 36, they had quite an influx of those dried out farmers come in here from Nebraska and Wyoming and Dakotas. There was quite a few people here then. Why do you think that it fluctuated? Other than that, why do you think that the population fluctuated quite a bit. People would come and try farming and find out they couldn't make it? Or why do you think they came and went? Well, I think the Depression was more anything. They'd come out here where they could at least have some wood and some oats and beans and raise a few vegetables. So then the economy probably is the way. The economy. That was during the Depression, you know. What do you remember about the 3C camps, CCC camps? There was quite a few CCs around this country. They wasn't right in this area here, but around Priest River and Sand Point, there was quite a few CCs. I was too young. I don't really remember too much about them. I was never in the CCs at all. Well, now, then you did say the other day that you were at Fort Lewis during World War II. No, I was at Farragut. Oh, at Farragut? Where did I put that? Fort Lewis. We must have mentioned something else about Fort Lewis. My dad was at Fort Lewis in 1918. Oh, maybe that's where I got that. I put in about six, seven months over here at Farragut. You were in the Navy or you were working? I was in the Navy. You were in the Navy. And I went from there to sea for about two years. Oh, you did? And then what year did you marry? We were married in 1947 in Spokane. And your wife's name is? Hazel. Hazel Larson? Larson. And we lived in Montana for about 20 years. I worked in the woods up there, logged up there in Montana, until 1966, I guess. Were some of your family still here at that time? My family still lived here, yeah. My mother and dad lived here. Then my dad died in 1965. We came back down here in 1966 then. Been here ever since. I've been here ever since. Have you ever thought about living some other place other than here in Montana? No. Do you think this has everything that you're looking for. Eberts Well, you never get rich here, but you can't starve to death here. Clendenin What do you think about the climate? Do you think it's the same year to year? Same year to year? No, it isn't the same year to year. I've seen some pretty warm winters here, and I saw some pretty cold ones. Some years we have a lot of snow, and some years we don't have any. I've seen five feet of snow here on the level some winters. Have you been snowed in then? No, we've been snowed in here a few times. Get the bulldozer and puff up. More likely to be mudded in, right? Yeah. Like this year. Ten years ago, these roads in this country, in the spring, you just forgot about going in there for about Six weeks. I can see that. When the people first came here to farm, did they try raising various kinds of crops? Or did they? I don't know just what they tried to raise here. But at one time, this place here And several others around here was all plotted up into five-acre lots. They had built a water tank over here and put in a big pump, and they was going to irrigate a lot of this area right in here. But it never ever materialized. They never ever got raised. They got the land all plowed out into five or ten acre lots, but they never ever sold any of them, I guess. HURST Who would have made those arrangements? It was two fellows came in here by the name of Prater and Coy. They bought, or it wasn't exactly bought, they just kind of got options on about three of these ranches around here and had them all blotted off. And then they never went through with it. And they reverted back to the original owners. But it isn't very good country to raise anything. And right here, it's too frosty. We get frost here nine months of the year. So Even potatoes, which they grow just over here a ways, don't do as well right in this particular spot. Well, I can't really say about potatoes, because Jim Judge down here, he's raised a lot of potatoes. And he said it was a really better potato country than farther south, because you didn't have to irrigate so much. But that wasn't one of the crops that the people raised when they first came. Mostly hay is all we've ever raised here, hay and some grain. They got electricity in this valley here in 1940 down three or four miles here. But then the rest of the country didn't get electricity until about 48, I think it was. Really, really late in my estimation. Why was it only in part of the area? I don't know why. We got electricity here in 40 and went up here about a mile. And then the rest of them didn't get it till about 48.
